compositions.name: Azure AI Foundry Agent Service
compositions.language: API (multi-language SDKs: Python, .NET, JavaScript, Java, REST)
compositions.intent: Fully managed Azure platform for building, deploying, and scaling AI agents that combine a Foundry-catalog model, instructions, and tools, with built-in identity, content safety, tracing, evaluation, and three agent shapes (prompt, workflow, hosted-container).
compositions.description: Foundry Agent Service hosts and scales agents on Azure-managed runtime. An agent is a model from the Foundry catalog plus instructions plus tools; the service handles tool execution, conversation state, Microsoft Entra identity, content filters, virtual-network isolation, tracing into Application Insights, and a build-test-deploy-monitor lifecycle with immutable versioning. Three agent types coexist: prompt agents (configuration-only), workflow agents (declarative YAML or visual designer for sequential / group-chat / human-in-the-loop orchestrations), and hosted agents (your container running Microsoft Agent Framework, LangGraph, Semantic Kernel, or custom code, per-session VM-isolated). The catalog of built-in tools includes Web Search, Code Interpreter, File Search, Azure AI Search, Function calling, OpenAPI tools, Image Generation, Browser Automation, Computer Use, plus MCP and A2A as custom tool surfaces.
primary_use_cases: no-code prompt agents in the Foundry portal
declarative multi-step workflows that orchestrate agents and business logic
containerized custom-code agents (Agent Framework / LangGraph / Semantic Kernel) on managed VMs
enterprise tool integration via MCP, OpenAPI, and Azure connectors
publishing agents to Microsoft 365, Teams, and the Entra Agent Registry
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Managed agent runtime around a Foundry-catalog model. Prompt agents run a single-agent tool-use loop: model reasons over instructions, the service decides whether to call a built-in tool (web search, code interpreter, file search, MCP, OpenAPI, function call), executes, observes, continues. Workflow agents bind that loop into a declarative graph of nodes (agent invocation, if/else, for-each, human-in-the-loop, group chat) defined in YAML or the visual designer. Hosted agents replace the inner loop with user code in a per-session VM-isolated sandbox that calls Foundry models, Toolbox MCP, and the Responses / Invocations / Activity / A2A protocols. Every run is traced, versioned, and gated by content filters and Entra identity.

key_concepts.name: Prompt / Workflow / Hosted agents
key_concepts.summary: Three agent shapes: prompt (configuration-only), workflow (declarative orchestration), hosted (your container on managed runtime).

key_concepts.name: Foundry tool catalog
key_concepts.summary: Built-in tools (web search, Code Interpreter, File Search, Azure AI Search, Functions) plus custom tools (MCP, OpenAPI, A2A) registered through the portal.

key_concepts.name: Workflows
key_concepts.summary: Visual / YAML orchestration with sequential, group-chat, and human-in-the-loop patterns.

key_concepts.name: Hosted agents
key_concepts.summary: Per-session VM-isolated containers with persistent $HOME and /files; Responses / Invocations / Activity / A2A protocols.

key_concepts.name: Guardrails and content filters
key_concepts.summary: Integrated content filters with cross-prompt-injection (XPIA) protection.

key_concepts.name: Agent identity and tracing
key_concepts.summary: Per-agent Microsoft Entra identity, RBAC, Application Insights tracing of every model call and tool invocation.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User request] --> entra[Microsoft Entra identity check]
  entra --> runtime[Foundry Agent Runtime]
  runtime --> kind{Agent type}
  kind -->|prompt| prompt[Prompt agent]
  kind -->|workflow| wf[Workflow YAML / visual graph]
  kind -->|hosted| host[Hosted container / VM sandbox]
  prompt --> loop[Tool-use loop]
  wf --> node[Node: agent / if-else / for-each / HITL]
  node --> loop
  host --> loop
  loop --> tools[Foundry tool catalog]
  tools --> websearch[Web Search]
  tools --> ci[Code Interpreter]
  tools --> fs[File Search vector store]
  tools --> ais[Azure AI Search]
  tools --> fn[Function calling]
  tools --> oapi[OpenAPI tool]
  tools --> mcp[MCP server / Toolbox]
  tools --> a2a[A2A endpoint]
  loop --> filter[Content filters / XPIA]
  filter --> trace[App Insights tracing]
  trace --> publish[Publish: stable endpoint / Teams / M365]

members.note: Hosted agents persist per-session $HOME and /files across idle / resume; sessions live up to 30 days with 15-minute idle timeout and automatic state restore.

evidence.title: Foundry hosted agents — session state persistence
evidence.quote: $HOME and /files content are persisted across turns and across idle periods. When compute goes idle and is brought back (on new or existing infrastructure), the session's state is automatically restored.

evidence.title: Foundry hosted agents — session lifetime
evidence.quote: Sessions persist for up to 30 days. The idle timeout is 15 minutes—if no request arrives within that window, the platform deprovisions the compute and persists the session state.

members.note: File Search vector store and Azure AI Search are first-class built-in tools an agent calls to ground responses in private documents.

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — File Search
evidence.quote: File Search — Augment agents with knowledge from uploaded files or proprietary documents by using vector search.

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — Azure AI Search
evidence.quote: Azure AI Search | Ground agents with data from an existing Azure AI Search index.

members.note: Workflow agents support explicit human-in-the-loop nodes; MCP tool config exposes a require_approval flag (e.g. 'always').

evidence.title: Foundry workflows — human-in-the-loop pattern
evidence.quote: Human in the loop | Asks the user a question and awaits user input to proceed | Creating approval requests during workflow execution and waiting for human approval

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — MCPTool require_approval
evidence.quote:
tool = MCPTool(
    server_label="github",
    server_url="https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp",
    require_approval="always",

members.note: Code Interpreter is a built-in tool that runs Python in a sandboxed environment for data analysis, math, and chart generation.

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — Code Interpreter
evidence.quote: Code Interpreter — Let agents write and run Python code in a sandboxed environment for data analysis, math, and chart generation.

members.note: Documented Evaluate step in the lifecycle; agent evaluators measure quality and safety regressions before publish.

evidence.title: Foundry development lifecycle — Evaluate step
evidence.quote: Evaluate — Run evaluations to measure quality and catch regressions.

evidence.title: Foundry development lifecycle — evaluation framing
evidence.quote: Before you publish your agent (and after any meaningful change), run evaluations to catch regressions and measure quality consistently across versions.

members.note: Integrated content filters on input and output; safety controls explicitly listed.

evidence.title: Foundry overview — content safety
evidence.quote: Content safety — Integrated content filters help mitigate prompt injection risks (including cross-prompt injection) and prevent unsafe outputs.

evidence.title: Foundry overview — safety controls
evidence.quote: Safety controls: Use integrated guardrails to help reduce unsafe outputs and mitigate prompt injection risks, including cross-prompt injection attacks (XPIA).

members.note: Remote and local MCP servers are first-class tools; Toolbox exposes a curated bundle as a single MCP-compatible endpoint.

evidence.title: Foundry overview — MCP servers
evidence.quote: Foundry supports remote MCP servers that you can add from the Add Tools catalog in the Foundry portal.

evidence.title: Foundry Toolbox — MCP endpoint
evidence.quote: A toolbox is a curated bundle of tools - such as web search, Azure AI Search, code interpreter, file search, MCP servers, and OpenAPI tools - that you configure once and expose as a single MCP-compatible endpoint.

members.note: Agents bind to a model from the Foundry catalog and can be swapped without changing agent code; many models supported (GPT-4o, Llama, DeepSeek).

evidence.title: Foundry overview — model swap
evidence.quote: Models | Works with many models from the Foundry model catalog, such as GPT-4o, Llama, and DeepSeek. Swap models without changing your agent code.

members.note: Workflow agents orchestrate multiple specialist agents; sequential / group-chat / branching are templated patterns.

evidence.title: Foundry workflows — multi-agent orchestration
evidence.quote: By orchestrating multiple agents, each powered by a Foundry model with specialized skills or roles, you can create systems that are more robust, adaptive, and capable of solving real-world problems collaboratively.

evidence.title: Foundry workflows — group chat pattern
evidence.quote: Group chat | Dynamically passes control between agents based on context or rules | Dynamic workflows, escalation, fallback, or expert handoff scenarios

members.note: Content filters explicitly mitigate cross-prompt-injection attacks (XPIA).

evidence.title: Foundry overview — XPIA mitigation
evidence.quote: Use integrated guardrails to help reduce unsafe outputs and mitigate prompt injection risks, including cross-prompt injection attacks (XPIA).

members.note: Agent nodes can be configured to return JSON Schema output via the workflow visualizer or API.

evidence.title: Foundry workflows — JSON schema output
evidence.quote: To configure an agent to return structured JSON output: In the Invoke agent configuration window, select Create a new agent. Configure the agent to send output as a JSON schema

members.note: Hosted agents run in per-session VM-isolated sandboxes; sessions are isolated from each other.

evidence.title: Foundry hosted agents — isolation model
evidence.quote: Hosted agents run in per-session VM-isolated sandboxes. Each session gets a dedicated sandbox with a persistent filesystem ($HOME and /files), enabling scale-to-zero with stateful resume and predictable cold starts. Sessions are isolated from each other

members.note: Tools are the headline primitive: built-in (Web Search, Code Interpreter, File Search, Azure AI Search, Functions, OpenAPI) and custom (MCP, A2A, Toolbox).

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — what a tool is
evidence.quote: A tool is a capability that an agent can invoke during a conversation to perform a specific task. When an agent receives a user message, the Foundry model powering the agent decides whether to call a tool based on the agent's instructions and the available tool definitions.

evidence.title: Foundry overview — tools listing
evidence.quote: Built-in tools including web search, file search, memory, code interpreter, MCP servers, and custom functions.

members.note: Computer Use and Browser Automation are listed built-in tools (both currently preview).

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — Computer Use
evidence.quote: Computer Use (preview) | Interact with computer systems through their user interfaces.

evidence.title: Foundry tool catalog — Browser Automation
evidence.quote: Browser Automation (preview) | Perform browser tasks through natural language prompts.

members.note: Group-chat workflow pattern dynamically routes between specialist agents; no explicit 'supervisor' vocabulary in the Foundry docs reviewed.

evidence.title: Foundry workflows — group chat handoff
evidence.quote: Group chat | Dynamically passes control between agents based on context or rules

references.title: What is Microsoft Foundry Agent Service?
Agent tools overview for Foundry Agent Service
Build a workflow in Microsoft Foundry
Hosted agents in Foundry Agent Service (preview)
Announcing General Availability of Azure AI Foundry Agent Service (May 2025)

compositions.name: Amazon Bedrock Agents
compositions.language: API (multi-language SDKs: Python, Java, JavaScript, .NET, Go via AWS SDKs)
compositions.intent: AWS-managed agent runtime that turns a Bedrock foundation model into a tool-using, knowledge-base-grounded agent: action groups (OpenAPI/function-detail schemas backed by Lambda or return-of-control), Bedrock Knowledge Bases for RAG, code interpretation, multi-agent supervisor/collaborator hierarchies, long-term memory, and guardrails.
compositions.description: Amazon Bedrock Agents is a fully managed runtime for autonomous agents on AWS. At build time you assemble a foundation model, natural-language instructions, one or more action groups (each defined by an OpenAPI or function-detail schema and resolved via a Lambda function or by returning control to the developer), and optional Bedrock Knowledge Bases. At runtime the InvokeAgent API drives a pre-processing -> orchestration -> post-processing sequence where the model generates a rationale, picks an action or knowledge-base query, observes the result, and iterates until done. Multi-agent collaboration designates a supervisor agent that routes work to collaborator agents. Memory retains conversational context across sessions. Bedrock Guardrails apply content filters, denied topics, PII redaction, and contextual grounding checks.
primary_use_cases: OpenAPI / function-detail action groups backed by Lambda
RAG-grounded enterprise assistants via Bedrock Knowledge Bases
code-interpreter data analysis and file processing
return-of-control for human or developer-side action execution
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Three-phase ReAct-style sequence per InvokeAgent request: (1) Pre-processing validates the user input; (2) Orchestration is a model-driven loop where the FM emits a rationale, predicts which action group to invoke or which knowledge base to query, the action runs (via Lambda or by returning control to the developer) or the KB is queried, the observation augments the base prompt, and the loop iterates; (3) Post-processing formats the final response. Conversation history persists per session, long-term memory summarises completed sessions per memoryId. Multi-agent collaboration wraps this loop with a supervisor that delegates to collaborator agents.

key_concepts.name: Action groups
key_concepts.summary: OpenAPI or function-detail schemas backed by a Lambda function (or return-of-control) that define the actions an agent can take.

key_concepts.name: Knowledge Bases
key_concepts.summary: Vector-backed RAG sources the agent queries to augment generation.

key_concepts.name: Orchestration loop
key_concepts.summary: Pre-processing -> rationale -> action / KB -> observation -> iterate, optional post-processing.

key_concepts.name: Return of control
key_concepts.summary: Action groups can return parameters to the caller instead of invoking Lambda, letting the developer execute and feed results back via invocationId.

key_concepts.name: Multi-agent collaboration
key_concepts.summary: Designate a supervisor agent and associate collaborator agents; supervisor builds and executes a plan across them.

key_concepts.name: Memory
key_concepts.summary: Long-term memory summarises sessions per memoryId; retained up to 365 days.

key_concepts.name: Code Interpretation
key_concepts.summary: Sandboxed code execution for data analysis, charting, and file processing.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User prompt] --> invoke[InvokeAgent]
  invoke --> pre[Pre-processing: validate input]
  pre --> orch[Orchestration loop]
  orch --> rationale[FM emits rationale]
  rationale --> pick{Pick}
  pick -->|action group| ag[OpenAPI / function-detail]
  ag --> exec{Executor}
  exec -->|Lambda| lambda[Lambda invocation]
  exec -->|return control| roc[Return-of-control to developer]
  pick -->|knowledge base| kb[Bedrock KB RAG]
  pick -->|code interpreter| ci[Sandboxed Python]
  lambda --> obs[Observation]
  roc --> obs
  kb --> obs
  ci --> obs
  obs --> done{Done?}
  done -->|no| orch
  done -->|yes| post[Post-processing]
  post --> guard[Bedrock Guardrails]
  guard --> reply[Reply to user]
  reply --> mem[Memory summarisation per memoryId]
  supervisor[Supervisor agent] -.delegates.-> orch
  supervisor -.coordinates.-> collab[Collaborator agents]

members.note: Session conversation history persists across InvokeAgent calls per sessionId; memoryId summaries restore long-term context across new sessions.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — session history preserved
evidence.quote: As the user session with the agent continues through more InvokeAgent requests, the conversation history is preserved. The conversation history continually augments the orchestration base prompt template with context

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — memory loads on next invocation
evidence.quote: the next time you invoke the agent with the same memoryId, the summary of each session stored in the memory gets loaded to the current session.

members.note: Knowledge Bases are associated with an agent; the orchestration step explicitly chooses between action invocation and KB query.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — KB augmentation
evidence.quote: Associate a knowledge base with the agent to augment the agent's performance.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — KB step in orchestration
evidence.quote: Query an associated knowledge base (Knowledge base response generation) to retrieve additional context and summarize the data to augment its generation.

members.note: Return of Control routes action parameters back to the developer's application instead of Lambda, enabling explicit external execution / approval gating.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — return of control mechanic
evidence.quote: Rather than sending the information that your agent has elicited from the user to a Lambda function for fulfillment, you can instead choose to return control to the agent developer by sending the information in the InvokeAgent response.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — RETURN_CONTROL config
evidence.quote: Through the API, you specify RETURN_CONTROL as the customControl value in the actionGroupExecutor object

members.note: Code interpretation generates and runs code in a secure sandbox; supports analysis and file processing.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — code interpretation
evidence.quote: The code interpretation enables your agent to generate, run, and troubleshoot your application code in a secure test environment.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents page — code interpretation
evidence.quote: Agents supports the ability to dynamically generate and execute code in a secure environment

members.note: Memory feature retains context across multiple sessions per memoryId with configurable retention up to 365 days.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents memory — across sessions
evidence.quote: Memory provides your agent the ability to retain conversational context across multiple sessions and to recall past actions and behaviors.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents memory — retention
evidence.quote: If memory is enabled, your agent retains the sessions in the memory for up to 365 days.

members.note: Multi-agent collaboration breaks tasks across specialist sub-agents under a supervisor; collaborators can also work in parallel.

evidence.title: Bedrock multi-agent — decomposition
evidence.quote: you can quickly assemble a team of agents that can break down tasks, assign specific tasks to domain specialist sub-agents, work in parallel

evidence.title: Bedrock multi-agent — plan and execute
evidence.quote: When you invoke the supervisor agent, it automatically creates and executes a plan across a set of collaborator agents and routes relevant requests and tasks to the appropriate collaborator agent.

members.note: Explicit supervisor agent designation; collaborators are associated with the supervisor and routed to by it.

evidence.title: Bedrock multi-agent — supervisor designation
evidence.quote: you can quickly designate an Amazon Bedrock Agent as the supervisor and then associate one or more collaborator agents with the supervisor.

evidence.title: Bedrock multi-agent — supervisor role
evidence.quote: Supervisor agent – Takes questions from the user, checks if the question is about the existing mortgage, new mortgage, or it is a general question and routes the question to the appropriate collaborator agent.

members.note: Action groups are the headline tool-use primitive: OpenAPI or function-detail schemas executed via Lambda or return-of-control.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — action groups
evidence.quote: Action groups – You define the actions that the agent should perform for the user (through providing the following resources): One of the following schemas to define the parameters that the agent needs to elicit from the user

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — OpenAPI / function-detail
evidence.quote: An OpenAPI schema to define the API operations that the agent can invoke to perform its tasks. The OpenAPI schema includes the parameters that need to be elicited from the user.

members.note: Action groups use OpenAPI / function-detail schemas to elicit typed parameters from the model; no first-class JSON-schema response mode documented for the final user reply.

evidence.title: Bedrock Agents — typed parameters
evidence.quote: A function detail schema to define the parameters that the agent can elicit from the user.

members.note: Bedrock Guardrails provide content filters, denied topics, PII redaction, and contextual grounding checks; applicable to agent inputs and outputs.

evidence.title: Bedrock Guardrails — overview
evidence.quote: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails provides configurable safeguards to help you build safe generative AI applications.

evidence.title: Bedrock Guardrails — filter types
evidence.quote: Content filters – This filter helps you detect and filter harmful text or image content in input prompts or model responses.

members.note: Bedrock Agents themselves use action groups (OpenAPI / Lambda), not MCP. MCP support is provided in the adjacent Bedrock AgentCore platform, not in this product surface as documented.

references.title: Automate tasks using Amazon Bedrock Agents (user guide)
Multi-agent collaboration with Amazon Bedrock Agents
Return control to the agent developer

compositions.name: Vertex AI Agent Builder
compositions.language: API (multi-language SDKs: Python, Java, Go, TypeScript via Agent Development Kit)
compositions.license: proprietary (managed service); Agent Development Kit is open-source (Apache-2.0)
compositions.intent: Google Cloud's end-to-end platform to build, scale, and govern agents: an open-source Agent Development Kit (ADK) for code-first multi-agent design, Vertex AI Agent Engine as the managed runtime with Sessions, Memory Bank, and Code Execution, plus Agent Garden samples and governance / observability hooks.
compositions.description: Vertex AI Agent Builder (now branded Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform) is Google Cloud's open platform for building, scaling, and governing agents. The build pillar is the Agent Development Kit (ADK) — an open-source framework in Python, Java, Go, and TypeScript that supports both code-defined custom agents and pre-built workflow agents (SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, LoopAgent), with native multi-agent composition (sub-agents, hierarchical delegation), tools (built-in, custom, MCP, OpenAPI), and the A2A protocol for agent-to-agent communication. The scale pillar is Vertex AI Agent Engine: a managed runtime with Sessions (conversation state), Memory Bank (long-term personalised memory), and Code Execution. Agent Garden ships curated samples for one-click deployment. Governance layers add Cloud Trace observability, IAM-based agent identity, and Security Command Center threat detection.
primary_use_cases: code-first multi-agent systems built with ADK and deployed on Agent Engine
workflow agents (sequential / parallel / loop) for predictable pipelines
long-running production agents with managed Sessions and Memory Bank
RAG agents grounded by Vertex AI Search and Vertex AI RAG Engine
agent-to-agent ecosystems over the open A2A protocol
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Two-tier shape. ADK defines the agent loop: an LlmAgent reasons over instructions and decides which tool or sub-agent to call, while workflow agents (SequentialAgent / ParallelAgent / LoopAgent) compose deterministic pipelines around LLM agents. Multi-agent hierarchies delegate by transferring control to sub-agents. The composed agent is then deployed onto Agent Engine, which provides the managed runtime, Sessions for conversation state, Memory Bank for persistent long-term memory, sandboxed Code Execution, and Cloud Trace observability. Tools span built-in, custom, OpenAPI, MCP, and A2A endpoints.

key_concepts.name: Agent Development Kit (ADK)
key_concepts.summary: Open-source agent framework in Python, Java, Go, TypeScript with native multi-agent composition.

key_concepts.name: Workflow agents
key_concepts.summary: SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, LoopAgent compose deterministic pipelines around LLM sub-agents.

key_concepts.name: Agent Engine
key_concepts.summary: Managed runtime that scales agents in production with Sessions, Memory Bank, and Code Execution.

key_concepts.name: Sessions
key_concepts.summary: Maintain conversation state across turns; managed by Agent Engine.

key_concepts.name: Memory Bank
key_concepts.summary: Persistent long-term memory for user preferences and facts across sessions.

key_concepts.name: A2A protocol
key_concepts.summary: Open agent-to-agent protocol; Agent2Agent integration is a first-class ADK concept.

key_concepts.name: Agent Garden
key_concepts.summary: Curated repository of agent samples, solutions, and tools with one-click deployment.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  dev[Developer] --> adk[Agent Development Kit]
  adk --> kind{Agent kind}
  kind -->|LlmAgent| llm[LLM agent reasoning]
  kind -->|SequentialAgent| seq[Sequential pipeline]
  kind -->|ParallelAgent| par[Parallel fan-out]
  kind -->|LoopAgent| loop[Loop until condition]
  llm --> tools[Tools]
  tools --> builtin[Built-in / Vertex AI Search]
  tools --> custom[Custom Python / Java]
  tools --> mcp[MCP server]
  tools --> a2a[A2A remote agent]
  llm --> sub[Sub-agents / hierarchy]
  seq --> llm
  par --> llm
  loop --> llm
  adk --> deploy[Deploy: adk deploy]
  deploy --> engine[Vertex AI Agent Engine runtime]
  engine --> sess[Sessions]
  engine --> mem[Memory Bank]
  engine --> code[Code Execution]
  engine --> trace[Cloud Trace observability]
  engine --> iam[IAM agent identity]

members.note: Agent Engine Sessions maintain conversation state across turns; sessions and memory bank are GA on Agent Engine.

evidence.title: Agent Engine — sessions
evidence.quote: Learn how to use sessions to maintain conversation state with your agents.

evidence.title: Agent Engine — conversation state
evidence.quote: Utilizing Agent Platform Memory Bank to maintain the state of continuous conversations and retrieve persistent, long-term memories

members.note: ADK includes a RAG Engine and Vertex AI Search integration; RAG overview, quickstart, and engine configuration are first-class ADK documentation areas.

evidence.title: ADK overview — RAG / Search integration
evidence.quote: Multiple RAG-related sections appear in the navigation menu, including "RAG overview," "RAG quickstart," and various RAG Engine deployment and configuration options

members.note: Agent Engine provides managed Code Execution so agents can run code safely.

evidence.title: Agent Engine — code execution
evidence.quote: Empowering agents to safely solve complex computational problems and automate workflows through dynamic Code Execution

members.note: Memory Bank stores long-term user preferences and facts across sessions; explicit Memory Bank API.

evidence.title: Agent Engine — Memory Bank purpose
evidence.quote: Learn how to use Memory Bank to store long-term user preferences and facts.

evidence.title: Agent Engine — Memory Bank API
evidence.quote: Get started with the Memory Bank API to manage long-term memories.

members.note: ADK ships SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, and LoopAgent for deterministic orchestration of sub-agents.

evidence.title: ADK — SequentialAgent
evidence.quote: Executes sub-agents one after another, in sequence.

evidence.title: ADK — ParallelAgent and LoopAgent
evidence.quote: Executes multiple sub-agents in parallel.

evidence.title: ADK overview — flexible orchestration
evidence.quote: Users can leverage flexible orchestration to define predictable pipelines using workflow agents or rely on agent-coordinated dynamic routing.

members.note: ADK natively supports multi-agent architectures with specialised teams that collaborate and delegate.

evidence.title: ADK — multi-agent architectures
evidence.quote: It natively supports multi-agent architectures, allowing developers to compose specialized teams of agents that can collaborate and delegate tasks.

members.note: ADK exposes a rich tool ecosystem: built-in tools, third-party integrations, and custom code; Google Search grounding included.

evidence.title: ADK — tool ecosystem
evidence.quote: ADK features a rich tool ecosystem for integrating third-party applications and your own custom code.

evidence.title: ADK docs — tool surface
evidence.quote: Supports "Custom Tools", "MCP tools", "OpenAPI tools"

members.note: ADK has a dedicated MCP section and ships MCP tool support.

evidence.title: ADK docs — MCP section
evidence.quote: Dedicated "MCP" section for Model Context Protocol integration

members.note: ADK supports structured output via Pydantic/JSON-schema configuration on LlmAgent; not surfaced as a headline doc on the overview pages reviewed.
Evaluation is a first-class ADK area: criteria, user simulation, custom metrics; partner evaluation tools and offline / online monitors.

evidence.title: ADK docs — evaluation
evidence.quote: Evaluation section covering Criteria, User Simulation, Custom Metrics

evidence.title: ADK overview — evaluation tools
evidence.quote: Apply built-in and partner evaluation tools to test execution trajectories

members.note: ADK exposes Callbacks as the design-pattern surface for guardrails; Safety and Security and Responsible AI are documented sections.

evidence.title: ADK docs — callbacks and safety
evidence.quote: Includes "Callbacks" with "Types of callbacks" and "design patterns"

evidence.title: ADK overview — Responsible AI
evidence.quote: Safety (Responsible AI) appears in the governance navigation menu

members.note: ADK is model-agnostic with adapters for many providers; can target Gemini, Claude, or other models.

evidence.quote: ADK can work with almost any generative AI model

evidence.quote: Provides adapters for "many other models and model providers"
